text: This is Dr. Craig Rogers, Director of Renal Surgery at the Vatakuta Urology Institute at Henry Ford Hospital. In this video, we demonstrate steps for bowel mobilization.

The surgeon incises the white line of Toldt with curved monopolar scissors, reflecting the bowel using sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The bowel is repositioned, revealing the avascular plane developed between the posterior mesocolon and Gerota's fascia.

The fourth robotic arm grasps the anterior Girotis fascia to retract the kidney anteriorly, aiding in bowel mobilization. The surgeon delicately dissects the brighter yellow mesenteric fat from the paler fat of the Girotis fascia.
The robotic grasping instrument spreads open the space between the mesentery and Girota's fascia, facilitating incision of the tissue while safeguarding underlying structures.

The surgeon exposes the upper pole of the kidney by mobilizing the spleen or liver, releasing the splenic flexure, pancreas, and splenic attachments to enhance the view of the renal hilum. The scissors are opened and used like a hook to carefully avoid trauma to the pancreas and splenic vessels.
The surgeon releases the splenic attachments, enabling the spleen to be reflected medially. The stomach, located behind the spleen, is carefully avoided to prevent injury during this maneuver.

The assistant uses a long tip suction to provide counter traction for releasing the upper pole attachments of the kidney. For right-sided tumors, passive liver retraction is performed using a locking grasper inserted through a five millimeter subxiphoid port, securing the liver to the diaphragm.
In the next video, we demonstrate the identification of important anatomical landmarks while performing robotic partial nephrectomy.
